scope: Deterministic offline provider outage, administrator-funded fallback authority, stuck-operation recovery, and deletion serialization

providerOutagePostgresJourney.transport: Global fetch stub; fixed provider URLs were observed but no network request was made

combinedPostgresGate.migrationCheck: Full checked-in migration chain through 0035 applied and was re-applied successfully

provedControls: Active learner credentials are attempted before administrator-funded credentials; corrupt credential ciphertext is isolated to its row and never aborts the remaining candidates.

Consent mutation, onboarding consent, fallback reservation, grant creation/revocation, provider-operation receipt acquisition, and both account-deletion transactions acquire the same global per-user advisory lock before narrower locks. They revalidate account/learner/receipt-owner status under that authority, and PostgreSQL races prove no consent, reservation, grant, or processing receipt is inserted after deletion wins and no reservation is authorized after withdrawal wins.

Grant creation is request-idempotent, rejects changed-payload UUID reuse and concurrent active learner/provider/model destinations, and commits grant, audit event, learner notification, and email outbox in one transaction.

Any route-authorized administrator can invoke the revocation command without a granted-by predicate. Revocation intentionally remains allowed during account shutdown because it only reduces authority; it still joins the global lock order and transactionally couples idempotent state, audit, notification, and outbox writes.
Fallback enumeration is deterministically ordered, bounded, and deduplicated by credential and model, so overlapping legacy grants cannot charge one learner request more than once.
A durable reservation row atomically guards both token and paise aggregates. Reconciliation is idempotent; missing, excessive, failed, or model-mismatched usage retains the full conservative charge.

Provider-reported model aliases are normalized under a strict provider-specific grammar; Google's documented models/ prefix is the only accepted prefix alias, while mismatches fail over as BAD_RESPONSE.

Provider-operation receipt acquisition takes the global owner lock and revalidates active status before lookup or insert, then begins an expiring lease with a monotonic version. Expired operations terminalize to a safe indeterminate response without re-executing the provider callback, and stale completions cannot pass the lease/version/expiry compare-and-set fence.

Grant authority and completed reservation fields are database-immutable. Migration 0034 rejects ordinary deletion even for an unused grant, while credential and reservation guards preserve referenced history. Explicit account deletion first rejects unreconciled calls, then uses a transaction-local authorization flag and deletes receipts, reservations, grants, and credentials in defined order.
Authored lesson blueprints and assessment banks, deterministic exam scoring, and progress aggregation remain usable during total permitted-provider outage.

Provider response bodies, unexpected errors, encrypted credential plaintext, and secret-like audit metadata do not enter learner responses, chat rows, model-call rows, notification metadata, or audit metadata.

auditImpact.AI-004: Adds linearizable fallback consent authority, idempotent and atomic grant commands, destination deduplication, strict model validation, and immutable dual-budget accounting
auditImpact.AI-009: All-permitted-provider outage and authored continuity path has deterministic zero-network PostgreSQL evidence
auditImpact.NFR-REL-001: Adds leased/fenced stuck-operation recovery, lost-response replay, bounded failover, and conservative reconciliation
auditImpact.NFR-PRV-002: Provider and administrator-fallback withdrawal is serialized against new fallback reservations
auditImpact.TM-AI-003: Cross-user credential, active-destination, hard-budget, and ledger-deletion controls have route, unit, and PostgreSQL coverage
auditImpact.TM-AI-008: Cross-provider consent, request replay, model-report validation, and unknown-outcome handling are fail-closed

notProved: Live provider health, teaching quality, actual served-model identity, real latency, real rate-limit semantics, or production outage recovery
Accuracy of administrator-entered pricing snapshots against provider invoices or foreign-exchange changes; the snapshot is a local authorization cap, not an invoice assertion
Production cookies, deployed routing, provider-side observability, or a deployed account-deletion/outage drill
Cross-request provider circuit breaking, automated provider-health decisions, or operator alert delivery
A multi-administrator PostgreSQL revocation journey; the release schema enforces a singleton active administrator, while the revocation command is structurally not bound to the grant creator
